rls.title: Row-Level Security on all app-data tables

soc2_cc.name: Logical access security software, infrastructure, and architectures over protected information assets
Authorize, modify, or remove access based on roles (least privilege / segregation of duties)

rls.rationale: RLS bounds what a role (e.g. anon) can read out of the database via the public/PostgREST API, mitigating bulk read of another tenant's rows from the data store (T1213) reachable through the exposed app surface (T1190); it is the technical access-restriction control A.8.3 / CC6.1.

revoke.title: REVOKE discipline on PII tables (default grants stripped from anon/public)

attack.name: Valid Accounts
attack.tactic: Initial Access, Persistence, Privilege Escalation, Stealth

soc2_cc.name: Authorize, modify, or remove access based on roles (least privilege / segregation of duties)
Logical access security software, infrastructure, and architectures over protected information assets

revoke.rationale: Stripping default grants from anon/public before any explicit grant removes the over-permissioned built-in role an attacker would otherwise ride (T1078) to read PII out of the repository (T1213); this is least-privilege provisioning, A.8.3/A.5.18 and CC6.3.

ssrf.title: SSRF protection on the scraping path (block internal/link-local egress)

attack.name: Unsecured Credentials: Cloud Instance Metadata API

soc2_cc.name: Logical access security measures against threats from outside the system boundaries
Logical access security software, infrastructure, and architectures over protected information assets

ssrf.rationale: Blocking server-side requests to localhost/link-local addresses (169.254.169.254) on the Firecrawl path denies the classic SSRF-to-cloud-metadata credential theft (T1552.005) and the broader exploit of the fetch endpoint (T1190); it is a boundary/egress control, A.8.26/A.8.20 and CC6.6.

webhook-auth.title: Inbound webhook authentication (HMAC-SHA256 + replay window)

soc2_cc.name: Restrict and protect the transmission and movement of information to authorized processes
Logical access security software, infrastructure, and architectures over protected information assets

webhook-auth.rationale: Verifying an HMAC-SHA256 signature with a replay window rejects forged/replayed payloads, defeating injection of tampered data in transit (T1565.002) by an actor impersonating the trusted sender (T1684.001); the cryptographic verification of an inbound transfer is A.8.24/A.8.26 and CC6.7.

dns-auth.title: Email domain authentication (SPF / DKIM / DMARC)

soc2_cc.name: Restrict and protect the transmission and movement of information to authorized processes
Logical access security software, infrastructure, and architectures over protected information assets

dns-auth.rationale: Well-formed SPF/DKIM/DMARC records on the sending domain prevent an adversary spoofing that domain (T1684.002, whose description explicitly cites SPF/DKIM/DMARC) to launch phishing (T1566); DKIM signing and authenticated transfer map to A.5.14/A.8.24 and CC6.7.

secret-leak.title: No secrets committed to the repository

soc2_cc.name: Logical access security software, infrastructure, and architectures over protected information assets
Authorize, modify, or remove access based on roles (least privilege / segregation of duties)

secret-leak.rationale: Keeping API keys and JWTs out of tracked files (.env gitignored) denies the exact credential-in-files theft of T1552.001; protecting this authentication information in the source tree is A.5.17/A.8.4 and the credential-management focus of CC6.1.

matrix-freshness.title: Bundled ATT&CK version matches MITRE's current Enterprise release

soc2_cc.name: Detection/monitoring to identify new vulnerabilities and configuration changes
Identify and analyze risks to the achievement of objectives

matrix-freshness.rationale: This is a meta/currency control, not an adversary behaviour, so attack is intentionally empty; keeping the bundled ATT&CK matrix in step with MITRE's published release is threat-intelligence currency (A.5.7) and the detection of newly-published technique coverage (CC7.1).

security-headers.title: Security response headers present (OWASP A05 — CSP / HSTS / X-Frame-Options / X-Content-Type-Options / Referrer-Policy)

soc2_cc.name: Logical access security measures against threats from outside the system boundaries
Detection/monitoring to identify new vulnerabilities and configuration changes

security-headers.rationale: Content-Security-Policy constrains script execution (mitigating injected JavaScript, T1059.007), HSTS forces TLS, and X-Frame-Options/X-Content-Type-Options/Referrer-Policy harden the browser against framing and content-type confusion used in drive-by compromise (T1189). Their presence in the delivered app's response is OWASP A05 misconfiguration hardening and the secure-configuration baseline A.8.26/A.8.9 and CC6.6.

dependency-audit.title: Dependency-CVE alerting configured (OWASP A06 — Dependabot/SCA enabled)

attack.name: Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools

soc2_cc.name: Detection/monitoring to identify new vulnerabilities and configuration changes
Identify and analyze risks to the achievement of objectives

dependency-audit.rationale: A configured dependency-CVE alerting pipeline (Dependabot or an SCA workflow) detects vulnerable/outdated components (OWASP A06) before a known CVE in a transitive dependency (T1195.001) becomes an exploitable path into the public-facing app (T1190); maintaining that pipeline is the technical-vulnerability management of A.8.8/A.8.25 and the new-vulnerability detection of CC7.1. NB: the handoff's 'reuse existing Dependabot check' was inaccurate — no such check existed; this is built fresh and also satisfies the logging-table 'Dependabot enabled' row.

app-logging.title: App-side security-event logging present (OWASP A09 — auth/security events logged)
app-logging.owasp: A09:2021 Security Logging and Monitoring Failures

attack.name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Cloud Logs

soc2_cc.name: Monitor system components for anomalies indicative of malicious acts
Evaluate security events to determine whether they could or did result in a failure

app-logging.rationale: An app that logs authentication and authorization-failure events leaves the audit trail an attacker would otherwise operate beneath; insufficient logging (OWASP A09) is the precondition the adversary relies on when defenses/logs are absent (T1562.008). Emitting those events is the logging and monitoring of A.8.15/A.8.16 and CC7.2/CC7.3. This is the app-side complement of the infra logging-config checks.

access-probe.title: Object-level access control enforced at the app layer (OWASP A01 — no IDOR)

soc2_cc.name: Logical access security software, infrastructure, and architectures over protected information assets
Authorize, modify, or remove access based on roles (least privilege / segregation of duties)

access-probe.rationale: Probing whether one authenticated user can reach another user's object by ID (IDOR) tests broken access control (OWASP A01) from outside the running app — the exploit of the public-facing API (T1190) to read another tenant's records (T1213). This is the SAME access-control boundary the repo-surface rls/revoke checks verify in policy; access-probe is the app-dynamic complement (the boundary tested as exercised), not a second source of truth — migration-policy correctness stays single-homed in rls.mjs/revoke.mjs. It is the access restriction of A.8.3/A.5.15 and CC6.1/CC6.3.

cookie-flags.title: Session cookie flags set (OWASP A07 — HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite)

attack.name: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Web Session Cookie

soc2_cc.name: Logical access security software, infrastructure, and architectures over protected information assets
Restrict and protect the transmission and movement of information to authorized processes

cookie-flags.rationale: HttpOnly keeps the session cookie out of reach of injected JavaScript, Secure binds it to TLS, and SameSite blocks cross-site send — together denying the theft (T1539) and replay (T1550.004) of the web session cookie (OWASP A07). Setting them is the secure-authentication control A.8.5/A.8.24 and the access/transmission protection of CC6.1/CC6.7.

supabase-logging.title: Supabase auth/API logging + log drain enabled (detection config)

attack.name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Cloud Logs

soc2_cc.name: Monitor system components for anomalies indicative of malicious acts
Evaluate security events to determine whether they could or did result in a failure

supabase-logging.rationale: Confirming Supabase auth/API logging (and a configured log drain where applicable) is on verifies the smoke detector is installed and powered — without it an adversary operates beneath the record (T1562.008). This is a presence-of-control config check: it asserts logging is enabled (A.8.15/A.8.16, CC7.2), not that anyone reads the logs (practice, owned by the companion doc).

gh-secret-scanning.title: GitHub secret scanning + push protection enabled (detection config)

soc2_cc.name: Detection/monitoring to identify new vulnerabilities and configuration changes
Logical access security software, infrastructure, and architectures over protected information assets

gh-secret-scanning.rationale: Secret scanning detects credentials committed to the repo (T1552.001) and push protection blocks them at commit time, narrowing a supply-chain foothold (T1195). Both being enabled is the source-code protection of A.8.4/A.8.28 and the detection control CC7.1. This is the detection-config complement of the repo-surface secret-leak check, which scans tracked content directly.

device-signin-alerts.title: Apex-identity new-device sign-in alerts enabled (Google + GitHub)

attack.name: Valid Accounts
attack.tactic: Initial Access, Persistence, Privilege Escalation, Stealth

soc2_cc.name: Monitor system components for anomalies indicative of malicious acts
Logical access security software, infrastructure, and architectures over protected information assets

device-signin-alerts.rationale: New-device sign-in alerts on the apex Google and GitHub identities surface a compromised or brute-forced account (T1078/T1110) the moment it is used from an unfamiliar device — anomaly monitoring (A.8.16, CC7.2) over the authentication information that gates everything downstream (A.5.17). Presence-of-control config check: alerts enabled, not whether anyone acts on them.

vercel-observability.title: Vercel observability + firewall configured (detection config)

soc2_cc.name: Monitor system components for anomalies indicative of malicious acts
Logical access security measures against threats from outside the system boundaries

vercel-observability.rationale: Vercel observability gives the request/runtime telemetry to spot exploitation of the public-facing app (T1190), and the firewall blunts edge abuse and denial of service (T1499) at the boundary. Both configured is the monitoring (A.8.16, CC7.2) and network-boundary protection (A.8.20, CC6.6) of the hosting surface. Presence-of-control config check.

alert-route.title: Alert route delivers a test event to the notification channel

iso27001_2022.name: Information security incident management planning and preparation

soc2_cc.name: Evaluate security events to determine whether they could or did result in a failure
Respond to identified security incidents

alert-route.rationale: This control closes the loop between detection and response: a test event must actually reach the alert channel for any of the logging-config controls to mean anything operationally (A.8.16/A.5.24, CC7.3/CC7.4). It is a delivery/plumbing control, not an adversary behaviour, so attack is intentionally empty (as with matrix-freshness). CURRENT STATE: the notification channel is STUBBED (notify/) pending an n8n workflow, so against the real route this check reports `unknown` by construction — an honest unverified, never a pass — until a test event is watched to arrive.
